content.text:
Memory stage: EntityCanonicalizer v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_c27434e8

        Stage instructions:
        You are EntityCanonicalizer v1.

Goal:
Resolve entity mentions from the latest turns into canonical entities.

Return JSON object:
{
  "operations": [
    {
      "mention": "text",
      "action": "link_existing | create_new | add_alias | noop",
      "entity_id": "uuid-or-null",
      "new_entity": {
        "entity_type": "user | person | agent | organization | project | repo | file | technology | product | location | concept | document | conversation | service | environment | other",
        "canonical_name": "text",
        "summary": "text-or-null"
      },
      "about_file_assertion": false,
      "alias_text": "text-or-null",
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "reason": "short explanation"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Prefer precision over recall.
- Do not merge distinct entities on weak evidence.
- Create new entities only when no candidate is a safe match.
- If a row in Candidate existing entities safely matches the mention, use "link_existing" with that exact row id.
- Use "add_alias" only when the mention is a new surface form for a safe row in Candidate existing entities.
- Relevant retrieval context can explain meaning, but it is not a linkable entity list. If it mentions a safe referent that is absent from Candidate existing entities, use "create_new" with the same canonical identity instead of inventing an entity_id.
- Canonical names are identity keys inside a namespace: the same canonical_name is treated as the same entity even if the entity_type differs.
- If distinct referents share the same surface form, disambiguate the canonical_name, for example "Java (programming language)" vs "Java (island)".
- If several candidates share a name, pick by entity type and summary; do not create another same-name entity just because the type differs.
- Prefer English for entity summaries; keep proper names, product names, repo names, and file names unchanged.
- Entity summaries must describe identity only, not mutable facts, current status, preferences, ownership, formats, fields, versions, or decisions. Put those facts into claims/notes instead.
- Create or link concrete personal items, personal copies, collected objects, signed items, kept media, or digital copies when later claims may need them as objects. First-person possessive wording such as "my X", "our X", "my copy of X", or "X in my collection" is direct evidence that X is a reusable item entity, even when the same sentence is also an event.
- For personal media or collected-item entities, use type "product" when the item is a concrete physical or digital artifact. The canonical name may be descriptive when no proper title is available, but it must identify the item well enough for later claim extraction.
- For USER first-person facts and preferences, resolve the user as the stable namespace-level USER entity named "User".
- For imported document/file_path/raw_url sources, the source path, URL, title, and section heading are source metadata. Do not create DOCUMENT, FILE, or URL-like entities merely to represent the imported source itself. Create a document/file entity only when the document content discusses that document/file as a durable domain object.
- For imported document sources, do not create FILE entities from incidental code references, stack traces, code blocks, file extensions, or paths such as "*.kt". Set action="noop" unless the text states a durable assertion about the file itself and later stages need that file as a claim subject or object.
- Set about_file_assertion=true only for FILE operations where the target text asserts something about that file itself. Set it false for non-FILE operations and incidental file-like mentions.
- Do not use raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" as entity_id.
- Do not invent placeholder ids such as "entity:<5>", "entity:<0>", "new_entity_1", or ids copied from retrieval context.
- entity_id must be an exact id from Candidate existing entities for "link_existing"/"add_alias", or null for "create_new"/"noop".
- Use relevant retrieval context to resolve ambiguous target mentions such as pronouns, "it", "that", "first one", "second one", and "from that list".
- When TARGET_MESSAGE selects one item from a previously retrieved ordered list, create or link only the selected concrete entity so later stages can write the target claim.
- Never create chat-specific USER entities such as "User of chat:..."; a new chat is not a new user.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Detected raw mentions:
        No deterministic mention detector is available in this MVP.
Infer only concrete reusable referents from the latest turns.
For first-person USER statements, create or link a stable USER entity when claims need a subject.

        Candidate existing entities:
        none

        Relevant retrieval context for ambiguous mention resolution:
        - source external:provided-text:37db411fb944863f6d52f658075d3cd0: LongMemEval past chat session.

Transcript:
user: I'm working on a model of a 1980s-era Ferrari 288 GTO, started it about three weeks ago on a Sunday afternoon, and I'm looking for some tips on how to achieve a more realistic paint finish.
assistant: What a great project! The Ferrari 288 GTO is an iconic supercar of the 80s, and achieving a realistic paint finish will make your model truly stand out. Here are some tips to help you achieve a stunning paint job:

1. **Research, research, research**: Study reference images of the real Ferrari 288 GTO to understand the color, finish, and subtle nuances of the paint job. Look for high-quality photos, both online and in books, to get a sense of the car's Rosso Corsa red paint.
2. **Choose the right paint**: Select a high-quality acrylic or enamel paint specifically designed for modeling. You can use either a Ferrari-specific red paint or a generic red paint that you can mix to match the correct shade. Make sure to read reviews and follow the manufacturer's instructions.
3. **Prepare the surface**: Ensure the model's surface is smooth, clean, and free of imperfections. Sand the entire model to create a uniform base for painting. Use progressively finer grit sandpaper (up to 4000) to achieve a high-gloss finish.
4. **Prime the model**: Apply a primer to create a uniform base coat and to help the paint adhere. Use a gray or white primer to help bring out the red color. Make sure the primer is fully dry before moving on to the next step.
5. **Apply the paint**: Use an airbrush or a high-quality paintbrush to apply thin, even coats of paint. Start with a light coat and gradually build up to the desired color intensity. Use a wet-sanding technique between coats to achieve a smooth finish.
6. **Layering and blending**: To achieve a deep, rich red color, apply multiple thin coats of paint, allowing each coat to dry before applying the next. Blend the paint edges carefully to avoid harsh transitions.
7. **Clear coat**: Apply a clear coat to protect the paint and give it a glossy finish. Use a clear acrylic or enamel clear coat specifically designed for modeling. Apply multiple thin coats, allowing each coat to dry before applying the next.
8. **Weathering and wear**: To add realism, consider adding subtle weathering effects, such as minor chips, scratches, or fading, using specialized weathering products or techniques.
9. **Reference, reference, reference**: Continuously refer to your reference images to ensure your paint job is accurate and realistic.
10. **Patience and practice**: Achieving a realistic paint finish takes time, patience, and practice. Don't be afraid to experiment, and don't rush the process.

Additional tips:

* Use a paint booth or a well-ventilated area to minimize dust and particles that can affect the paint finish.
* Invest in a good airbrush or paintbrush, as these will help you achieve a smoother, more consistent finish.
* Consider using a paint mask or decals for the iconic Ferrari stripes and logos to ensure accuracy and crisp lines.
* If you're new to painting models, consider practicing on a smaller, less prominent area first to get a feel for the paint and techniques.

By following these tips, you'll be well on your way to achieving a stunning, realistic paint finish on your Ferrari 288 GTO model. Good luck, and have fun!
user: I actually just got a new airbrush compressor recently, so I'm excited to try it out on this project. I've been reading up on some YouTube tutorials and forums on weathering and aging effects, and I think it'll really make a difference.
assistant: That's awesome! Congratulations on the new airbrush compressor! You're going to love using it on this project.

Weathering and aging effects can really take your model to the next level, adding a level of realism and character to the finish. It's great that you're doing your research and learning from YouTube tutorials and forums. There are some incredibly talented modelers out there who share their techniques and expertise online.

Some tips to keep in mind when weathering and aging your Ferrari 288 GTO model:

1. **Start subtle**: It's easy to overdo it with weathering effects, so start with subtle, gentle applications and build up gradually.
2. **Reference, reference, reference**: Study reference images of real cars to see how they age and wear in different environments. This will help you create a more realistic weathering effect.
3. **Use a variety of techniques**: Don't rely on a single technique or product. Experiment with different methods, such as washes, drybrushing, and pigments, to create a more nuanced and realistic effect.
4. **Focus on high-wear areas**: Pay attention to areas that would naturally show more wear, such as the wheels, tires, and exhaust pipes.
5. **Practice makes perfect**: Don't be discouraged if your first attempts at weathering don't turn out as expected. Keep practicing, and you'll develop your skills and style.

Some popular weathering products and techniques for models include:

1. **Washes**: Thinned paints or specialized wash products that can be applied to recessed areas to create shadow and depth.
2. **Drybrushing**: Using a almost-dry brush to apply paint to raised areas, creating a textured, worn look.
3. **Pigments**: Fine powders that can be applied to create subtle, realistic dirt and grime effects.
4. **Chipping and scratching**: Using a small brush or tool to create realistic chips and scratches in the paint.

Remember to have fun and be patient with the process. Weathering and aging effects can be time-consuming, but the end result will be worth it.

How's your airbrush compressor working out for you so far? Have you had a chance to test it out on any other projects or is this your first time using it?
user: I've only experimented with it on some test pieces, but I'm really happy with the results. The compressor has been a game-changer for me, allowing me to achieve much smoother, more even coats of paint. I'm excited to use it on my Ferrari project. Speaking of which, I recently applied decals to the engine compartment and body, and I'm really happy with how they turned out.
assistant: That's great to hear! It's always exciting to try out new tools and techniques, and it sounds like your airbrush compressor is going to make a big difference in your work.

Applying decals can be a bit nerve-wracking, but it sounds like you did a great job on the engine compartment and body. Decals can really add a lot of detail and realism to a model, and it's great that you're happy with how they turned out.

What kind of decals did you use? Were they pre-made or did you create your own? And how did you find the application process? Was it straightforward or were there any challenges you had to overcome?

Also, now that you've got the decals applied, what's next on your agenda for the Ferrari project? Are you moving on to the paint job, or are there other details you want to focus on first?

By the way, have you considered adding any additional details to the engine compartment, such as wiring, hoses, or other engine components? Sometimes, adding those small details can really make the model pop and give it a more realistic look.
user: I used pre-made decals specifically designed for the Ferrari 288 GTO. The application process was pretty straightforward, but it did take some patience and care to avoid air bubbles and wrinkles. I applied them to the engine compartment and body about a week ago, and I'm really happy with how they turned out.

Next, I'm actually planning to focus on the paint job, using my new airbrush compressor to get a smooth, even finish. I've been doing some research on weathering and aging effects, so I might try to add some subtle wear and tear to the model to give it a more realistic look.

Regarding additional details, that's a great point! I did think about adding some wiring and hoses to the engine compartment, but I wasn't sure if it would be worth the extra effort. Now that you mention it, I think it could really make a difference in the overall look of the model. Do you have any tips on how to add those details without making the model look
assistant: Using pre-made decals specifically designed for the Ferrari 288 GTO can definitely make the process easier and ensure accuracy.

It's great that you're planning to focus on the paint job next, and using your new airbrush compressor will definitely help you achieve a smooth, even finish. Weathering and aging effects can add a lot of character to the model, and subtle wear and tear can make it look more realistic.

Adding wiring and hoses to the engine compartment can definitely elevate the model's detail and realism. Here are some tips to help you add those details without making the model look cluttered:

1. **Reference, reference, reference**: Study reference images of the real Ferrari 288 GTO's engine compartment to get an idea of the wiring and hose layout.
2. **Use fine wire and thread**: Use thin wire (e.g., 0.5mm or 1mm) or thread to create the wires and hoses. This will help them blend in with the surrounding details.
3. **Keep it subtle**: Don't overdo it with the wiring and hoses. Focus on the most prominent ones and leave out the smaller details.
4. **Use a wire stripper**: Strip the insulation from the wire to create a more realistic look.
5. **Paint and weather**: Paint the wires and hoses to match the surrounding colors, and then weather them to give them a worn, aged look.
6. **Use a micro-brush**: Use a micro-brush to paint and weather the small details, as it will give you more control and precision.
7. **Add them in stages**: Add the wires and hoses in stages, allowing each stage to dry before adding more. This will prevent clutter and make it easier to work with.

Remember, the goal is to add subtle details that enhance the model's realism without overwhelming the senses. By following these tips, you can add wiring and hoses that look like they belong in the engine compartment.

How do you plan to approach the weathering and aging effects on the model? Are you going to use washes, drybrushing, or pigments?
user: I'm thinking of using a combination of washes and drybrushing to achieve a subtle weathering effect. I've seen some great tutorials online that demonstrate how to create a realistic worn look using these techniques. I'm also considering using pigments to add some subtle dirt and grime effects to the model.

By the way, I recently attended a local model building club meetup, and I met some fellow enthusiasts who showed me their incredible models. One of the guys, John, had an amazing WWII-era bomber model that was incredibly detailed. It was really inspiring to see the level of detail he had achieved, and it motivated me to push myself to add more complexity to my own models.
assistant: That's a great approach to weathering and aging! Using a combination of washes and drybrushing can create a very realistic worn look, and adding pigments can give it an extra layer of depth and realism. Washes are great for creating subtle, nuanced shading and color variations, while drybrushing can add a more textured, worn look to raised surfaces. Pigments can be used to add subtle dirt and grime effects, and can be blended to create a very realistic look.

It's great that you attended a local model building club meetup! Meeting fellow enthusiasts and seeing their work can be incredibly inspiring and motivating. It's amazing how much talent and expertise is out there, and it's always exciting to see what others are working on.

That WWII-era bomber model sounds incredible! It's great that it motivated you to push yourself to add more complexity to your own models. Sometimes, seeing what others are capable of can spark new ideas and inspiration, and it's awesome that you're feeling motivated to take your skills to the next level.

What specific areas of your Ferrari model do you think you'll focus on adding more complexity to? Are you thinking of adding more details to the engine, interior, or exterior?

Also, did you learn any new techniques or tips from the meetup that you're excited to try out on your current project?
user: I think I'll focus on adding more complexity to the engine compartment and interior. I've been thinking about adding more details like wiring, hoses, and maybe even some engine components like cylinders or pistons. I've also been considering adding more details to the interior, like textured seats or a more detailed dashboard.

As for new techniques, I did learn about a new way to create realistic paint chipping using a combination of masking tape and paint. One of the guys at the meetup showed me how to use the tape to create sharp, defined edges and then paint over it to create a chipped, worn look. I'm excited to try it out on my Ferrari model.

Speaking of which, I should mention that I started building this model about three weeks ago, on a Sunday afternoon. It was a rainy day, and I spent around 5 hours working on it, getting the basic chassis and engine block assembled. Since then, I've worked on it sporadically, usually for about an
assistant: Adding more complexity to the engine compartment and interior can really make your Ferrari model stand out. Wiring, hoses, and engine components like cylinders or pistons can add a lot of visual interest and realism to the model. And detailing the interior with textured seats and a more detailed dashboard can make the model feel more immersive and authentic.

That paint chipping technique sounds like a great way to add some realism to your model. Masking tape can be a really useful tool for creating sharp, defined edges and lines, and using it to create a chipped, worn look can add a lot of character to the model. I'm sure it'll be a great addition to your Ferrari.

It's great that you've been working on your model sporadically over the past three weeks. Consistency is key when it comes to making progress on a project, and it's awesome that you've been able to dedicate some time to it each week. Five hours on a Sunday afternoon is a great chunk of time to get started on a project, and it's impressive that you've been able to make progress on it since then.

How many hours do you think you've put into the model so far? And do you have a rough estimate of how many hours you think it'll take to complete the project?

Also, have you thought about displaying your model once it's complete? Do you have a display case or shelf in mind, or are you planning to enter it into any model shows or competitions?

responseFormat.description: Resolve target message mentions into canonical memory entities.
responseFormat.name: memory_entity_canonicalizer

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X

- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: EntityCanonicalizer v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_c27434e8

        Stage instructions:
        You are EntityCanonicalizer v1.

Goal:
Resolve entity mentions from the latest turns into canonical entities.

Return JSON object:
{
  "operations": [
    {
      "mention": "text",
      "action": "link_existing | create_new | add_alias | noop",
      "entity_id": "uuid-or-null",
      "new_entity": {
        "entity_type": "user | person | agent | organization | project | repo | file | technology | product | location | concept | document | conversation | service | environment | other",
        "canonical_name": "text",
        "summary": "text-or-null"
      },
      "about_file_assertion": false,
      "alias_text": "text-or-null",
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "reason": "short explanation"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Prefer precision over recall.
- Do not merge distinct entities on weak evidence.
- Create new entities only when no candidate is a safe match.
- If a row in Candidate existing entities safely matches the mention, use "link_existing" with that exact row id.
- Use "add_alias" only when the mention is a new surface form for a safe row in Candidate existing entities.
- Relevant retrieval context can explain meaning, but it is not a linkable entity list. If it mentions a safe referent that is absent from Candidate existing entities, use "create_new" with the same canonical identity instead of inventing an entity_id.
- Canonical names are identity keys inside a namespace: the same canonical_name is treated as the same entity even if the entity_type differs.
- If distinct referents share the same surface form, disambiguate the canonical_name, for example "Java (programming language)" vs "Java (island)".
- If several candidates share a name, pick by entity type and summary; do not create another same-name entity just because the type differs.
- Prefer English for entity summaries; keep proper names, product names, repo names, and file names unchanged.
- Entity summaries must describe identity only, not mutable facts, current status, preferences, ownership, formats, fields, versions, or decisions. Put those facts into claims/notes instead.
- Create or link concrete personal items, personal copies, collected objects, signed items, kept media, or digital copies when later claims may need them as objects. First-person possessive wording such as "my X", "our X", "my copy of X", or "X in my collection" is direct evidence that X is a reusable item entity, even when the same sentence is also an event.
- For personal media or collected-item entities, use type "product" when the item is a concrete physical or digital artifact. The canonical name may be descriptive when no proper title is available, but it must identify the item well enough for later claim extraction.
- For USER first-person facts and preferences, resolve the user as the stable namespace-level USER entity named "User".
- For imported document/file_path/raw_url sources, the source path, URL, title, and section heading are source metadata. Do not create DOCUMENT, FILE, or URL-like entities merely to represent the imported source itself. Create a document/file entity only when the document content discusses that document/file as a durable domain object.
- For imported document sources, do not create FILE entities from incidental code references, stack traces, code blocks, file extensions, or paths such as "*.kt". Set action="noop" unless the text states a durable assertion about the file itself and later stages need that file as a claim subject or object.
- Set about_file_assertion=true only for FILE operations where the target text asserts something about that file itself. Set it false for non-FILE operations and incidental file-like mentions.
- Do not use raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" as entity_id.
- Do not invent placeholder ids such as "entity:<5>", "entity:<0>", "new_entity_1", or ids copied from retrieval context.
- entity_id must be an exact id from Candidate existing entities for "link_existing"/"add_alias", or null for "create_new"/"noop".
- Use relevant retrieval context to resolve ambiguous target mentions such as pronouns, "it", "that", "first one", "second one", and "from that list".
- When TARGET_MESSAGE selects one item from a previously retrieved ordered list, create or link only the selected concrete entity so later stages can write the target claim.
- Never create chat-specific USER entities such as "User of chat:..."; a new chat is not a new user.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Detected raw mentions:
        No deterministic mention detector is available in this MVP.
Infer only concrete reusable referents from the latest turns.
For first-person USER statements, create or link a stable USER entity when claims need a subject.

        Candidate existing entities:
        none

        Relevant retrieval context for ambiguous mention resolution:
        - source external:provided-text:37db411fb944863f6d52f658075d3cd0: LongMemEval past chat session.

Transcript:
user: I'm working on a model of a 1980s-era Ferrari 288 GTO, started it about three weeks ago on a Sunday afternoon, and I'm looking for some tips on how to achieve a more realistic paint finish.
assistant: What a great project! The Ferrari 288 GTO is an iconic supercar of the 80s, and achieving a realistic paint finish will make your model truly stand out. Here are some tips to help you achieve a stunning paint job:

1. **Research, research, research**: Study reference images of the real Ferrari 288 GTO to understand the color, finish, and subtle nuances of the paint job. Look for high-quality photos, both online and in books, to get a sense of the car's Rosso Corsa red paint.
2. **Choose the right paint**: Select a high-quality acrylic or enamel paint specifically designed for modeling. You can use either a Ferrari-specific red paint or a generic red paint that you can mix to match the correct shade. Make sure to read reviews and follow the manufacturer's instructions.
3. **Prepare the surface**: Ensure the model's surface is smooth, clean, and free of imperfections. Sand the entire model to create a uniform base for painting. Use progressively finer grit sandpaper (up to 4000) to achieve a high-gloss finish.
4. **Prime the model**: Apply a primer to create a uniform base coat and to help the paint adhere. Use a gray or white primer to help bring out the red color. Make sure the primer is fully dry before moving on to the next step.
5. **Apply the paint**: Use an airbrush or a high-quality paintbrush to apply thin, even coats of paint. Start with a light coat and gradually build up to the desired color intensity. Use a wet-sanding technique between coats to achieve a smooth finish.
6. **Layering and blending**: To achieve a deep, rich red color, apply multiple thin coats of paint, allowing each coat to dry before applying the next. Blend the paint edges carefully to avoid harsh transitions.
7. **Clear coat**: Apply a clear coat to protect the paint and give it a glossy finish. Use a clear acrylic or enamel clear coat specifically designed for modeling. Apply multiple thin coats, allowing each coat to dry before applying the next.
8. **Weathering and wear**: To add realism, consider adding subtle weathering effects, such as minor chips, scratches, or fading, using specialized weathering products or techniques.
9. **Reference, reference, reference**: Continuously refer to your reference images to ensure your paint job is accurate and realistic.
10. **Patience and practice**: Achieving a realistic paint finish takes time, patience, and practice. Don't be afraid to experiment, and don't rush the process.

Additional tips:

* Use a paint booth or a well-ventilated area to minimize dust and particles that can affect the paint finish.
* Invest in a good airbrush or paintbrush, as these will help you achieve a smoother, more consistent finish.
* Consider using a paint mask or decals for the iconic Ferrari stripes and logos to ensure accuracy and crisp lines.
* If you're new to painting models, consider practicing on a smaller, less prominent area first to get a feel for the paint and techniques.

By following these tips, you'll be well on your way to achieving a stunning, realistic paint finish on your Ferrari 288 GTO model. Good luck, and have fun!
user: I actually just got a new airbrush compressor recently, so I'm excited to try it out on this project. I've been reading up on some YouTube tutorials and forums on weathering and aging effects, and I think it'll really make a difference.
assistant: That's awesome! Congratulations on the new airbrush compressor! You're going to love using it on this project.

Weathering and aging effects can really take your model to the next level, adding a level of realism and character to the finish. It's great that you're doing your research and learning from YouTube tutorials and forums. There are some incredibly talented modelers out there who share their techniques and expertise online.

Some tips to keep in mind when weathering and aging your Ferrari 288 GTO model:

1. **Start subtle**: It's easy to overdo it with weathering effects, so start with subtle, gentle applications and build up gradually.
2. **Reference, reference, reference**: Study reference images of real cars to see how they age and wear in different environments. This will help you create a more realistic weathering effect.
3. **Use a variety of techniques**: Don't rely on a single technique or product. Experiment with different methods, such as washes, drybrushing, and pigments, to create a more nuanced and realistic effect.
4. **Focus on high-wear areas**: Pay attention to areas that would naturally show more wear, such as the wheels, tires, and exhaust pipes.
5. **Practice makes perfect**: Don't be discouraged if your first attempts at weathering don't turn out as expected. Keep practicing, and you'll develop your skills and style.

Some popular weathering products and techniques for models include:

1. **Washes**: Thinned paints or specialized wash products that can be applied to recessed areas to create shadow and depth.
2. **Drybrushing**: Using a almost-dry brush to apply paint to raised areas, creating a textured, worn look.
3. **Pigments**: Fine powders that can be applied to create subtle, realistic dirt and grime effects.
4. **Chipping and scratching**: Using a small brush or tool to create realistic chips and scratches in the paint.

Remember to have fun and be patient with the process. Weathering and aging effects can be time-consuming, but the end result will be worth it.

How's your airbrush compressor working out for you so far? Have you had a chance to test it out on any other projects or is this your first time using it?
user: I've only experimented with it on some test pieces, but I'm really happy with the results. The compressor has been a game-changer for me, allowing me to achieve much smoother, more even coats of paint. I'm excited to use it on my Ferrari project. Speaking of which, I recently applied decals to the engine compartment and body, and I'm really happy with how they turned out.
assistant: That's great to hear! It's always exciting to try out new tools and techniques, and it sounds like your airbrush compressor is going to make a big difference in your work.

Applying decals can be a bit nerve-wracking, but it sounds like you did a great job on the engine compartment and body. Decals can really add a lot of detail and realism to a model, and it's great that you're happy with how they turned out.

What kind of decals did you use? Were they pre-made or did you create your own? And how did you find the application process? Was it straightforward or were there any challenges you had to overcome?

Also, now that you've got the decals applied, what's next on your agenda for the Ferrari project? Are you moving on to the paint job, or are there other details you want to focus on first?

By the way, have you considered adding any additional details to the engine compartment, such as wiring, hoses, or other engine components? Sometimes, adding those small details can really make the model pop and give it a more realistic look.
user: I used pre-made decals specifically designed for the Ferrari 288 GTO. The application process was pretty straightforward, but it did take some patience and care to avoid air bubbles and wrinkles. I applied them to the engine compartment and body about a week ago, and I'm really happy with how they turned out.

Next, I'm actually planning to focus on the paint job, using my new airbrush compressor to get a smooth, even finish. I've been doing some research on weathering and aging effects, so I might try to add some subtle wear and tear to the model to give it a more realistic look.

Regarding additional details, that's a great point! I did think about adding some wiring and hoses to the engine compartment, but I wasn't sure if it would be worth the extra effort. Now that you mention it, I think it could really make a difference in the overall look of the model. Do you have any tips on how to add those details without making the model look
assistant: Using pre-made decals specifically designed for the Ferrari 288 GTO can definitely make the process easier and ensure accuracy.

It's great that you're planning to focus on the paint job next, and using your new airbrush compressor will definitely help you achieve a smooth, even finish. Weathering and aging effects can add a lot of character to the model, and subtle wear and tear can make it look more realistic.

Adding wiring and hoses to the engine compartment can definitely elevate the model's detail and realism. Here are some tips to help you add those details without making the model look cluttered:

1. **Reference, reference, reference**: Study reference images of the real Ferrari 288 GTO's engine compartment to get an idea of the wiring and hose layout.
2. **Use fine wire and thread**: Use thin wire (e.g., 0.5mm or 1mm) or thread to create the wires and hoses. This will help them blend in with the surrounding details.
3. **Keep it subtle**: Don't overdo it with the wiring and hoses. Focus on the most prominent ones and leave out the smaller details.
4. **Use a wire stripper**: Strip the insulation from the wire to create a more realistic look.
5. **Paint and weather**: Paint the wires and hoses to match the surrounding colors, and then weather them to give them a worn, aged look.
6. **Use a micro-brush**: Use a micro-brush to paint and weather the small details, as it will give you more control and precision.
7. **Add them in stages**: Add the wires and hoses in stages, allowing each stage to dry before adding more. This will prevent clutter and make it easier to work with.

Remember, the goal is to add subtle details that enhance the model's realism without overwhelming the senses. By following these tips, you can add wiring and hoses that look like they belong in the engine compartment.

How do you plan to approach the weathering and aging effects on the model? Are you going to use washes, drybrushing, or pigments?
user: I'm thinking of using a combination of washes and drybrushing to achieve a subtle weathering effect. I've seen some great tutorials online that demonstrate how to create a realistic worn look using these techniques. I'm also considering using pigments to add some subtle dirt and grime effects to the model.

By the way, I recently attended a local model building club meetup, and I met some fellow enthusiasts who showed me their incredible models. One of the guys, John, had an amazing WWII-era bomber model that was incredibly detailed. It was really inspiring to see the level of detail he had achieved, and it motivated me to push myself to add more complexity to my own models.
assistant: That's a great approach to weathering and aging! Using a combination of washes and drybrushing can create a very realistic worn look, and adding pigments can give it an extra layer of depth and realism. Washes are great for creating subtle, nuanced shading and color variations, while drybrushing can add a more textured, worn look to raised surfaces. Pigments can be used to add subtle dirt and grime effects, and can be blended to create a very realistic look.

It's great that you attended a local model building club meetup! Meeting fellow enthusiasts and seeing their work can be incredibly inspiring and motivating. It's amazing how much talent and expertise is out there, and it's always exciting to see what others are working on.

That WWII-era bomber model sounds incredible! It's great that it motivated you to push yourself to add more complexity to your own models. Sometimes, seeing what others are capable of can spark new ideas and inspiration, and it's awesome that you're feeling motivated to take your skills to the next level.

What specific areas of your Ferrari model do you think you'll focus on adding more complexity to? Are you thinking of adding more details to the engine, interior, or exterior?

Also, did you learn any new techniques or tips from the meetup that you're excited to try out on your current project?
user: I think I'll focus on adding more complexity to the engine compartment and interior. I've been thinking about adding more details like wiring, hoses, and maybe even some engine components like cylinders or pistons. I've also been considering adding more details to the interior, like textured seats or a more detailed dashboard.

As for new techniques, I did learn about a new way to create realistic paint chipping using a combination of masking tape and paint. One of the guys at the meetup showed me how to use the tape to create sharp, defined edges and then paint over it to create a chipped, worn look. I'm excited to try it out on my Ferrari model.

Speaking of which, I should mention that I started building this model about three weeks ago, on a Sunday afternoon. It was a rainy day, and I spent around 5 hours working on it, getting the basic chassis and engine block assembled. Since then, I've worked on it sporadically, usually for about an
assistant: Adding more complexity to the engine compartment and interior can really make your Ferrari model stand out. Wiring, hoses, and engine components like cylinders or pistons can add a lot of visual interest and realism to the model. And detailing the interior with textured seats and a more detailed dashboard can make the model feel more immersive and authentic.

That paint chipping technique sounds like a great way to add some realism to your model. Masking tape can be a really useful tool for creating sharp, defined edges and lines, and using it to create a chipped, worn look can add a lot of character to the model. I'm sure it'll be a great addition to your Ferrari.

It's great that you've been working on your model sporadically over the past three weeks. Consistency is key when it comes to making progress on a project, and it's awesome that you've been able to dedicate some time to it each week. Five hours on a Sunday afternoon is a great chunk of time to get started on a project, and it's impressive that you've been able to make progress on it since then.

How many hours do you think you've put into the model so far? And do you have a rough estimate of how many hours you think it'll take to complete the project?

Also, have you thought about displaying your model once it's complete? Do you have a display case or shelf in mind, or are you planning to enter it into any model shows or competitions?
